label: AI Use Cases & Problem Types — Part A
topic: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML

questions.stem: When is AI a good choice for solving a business problem?

options.text: When the problem involves analyzing vast amounts of data at high velocity, recognizing patterns, or performing repetitive tasks that humans find tedious
When a simple rule-based if/then statement can solve the problem
When the cost of building the AI solution exceeds the expected business benefit
When deterministic output is the primary requirement

explanation.correct: AI excels at analyzing vast data at high velocity, recognizing patterns (fraud detection), detecting deviations (anomalies), and performing repetitive tasks. These are scenarios where humans cannot match AI's speed and consistency.

distractors.B: Simple rule-based problems don't need AI. A deterministic rule engine is simpler and more appropriate.
distractors.C: If costs exceed benefits, AI shouldn't be pursued. A cost-benefit analysis must show positive ROI.
distractors.D: ML models are probabilistic, not deterministic. If determinism is required, rule-based systems are better.

questions.stem: When should an organization NOT use AI?

options.text: When the costs of building the AI solution exceed the expected savings or benefits, or when deterministic output is required
When the problem involves large amounts of data
When pattern recognition would be valuable
When repetitive tasks need automation

explanation.correct: Don't use AI when costs exceed benefits (estimate fraud savings vs model cost) or when deterministic output is needed (ML is probabilistic). Also avoid AI when complete transparency is required but complex models are needed.

distractors.B: Large data volumes are actually where AI excels. This is a good use case for AI.
distractors.C: Pattern recognition is a core AI strength. This supports using AI, not avoiding it.
distractors.D: Repetitive task automation is ideal for AI. It frees employees for creative work.

questions.stem: What is the difference between deterministic and probabilistic systems?

options.text: Deterministic systems always produce the same output for the same input; ML models are probabilistic, producing varied results that incorporate randomness
Deterministic systems use neural networks; probabilistic systems use rules
They are the same thing with different names
Probabilistic systems are always more accurate than deterministic ones

explanation.correct: Deterministic systems (like rule-based applications) always produce the same output for the same input. ML models are probabilistic — they determine likelihood and incorporate randomness, so identical inputs may produce varied results.

distractors.B: This is reversed. Neural networks are probabilistic; rule-based systems are deterministic.
distractors.C: They are fundamentally different approaches. Deterministic = consistent; probabilistic = varied.
distractors.D: Accuracy depends on the problem. Deterministic rules may be perfectly accurate for simple problems.

questions.stem: What is binary classification?

options.text: Assigning an input to one of two predefined mutually exclusive classes based on its attributes
Assigning an input to one of many classes
Predicting a continuous numerical value
Grouping unlabeled data into clusters

explanation.correct: Binary classification assigns an input to one of two predefined, mutually exclusive classes. Examples include disease/no disease diagnosis, spam/not spam, or fish/not fish.

distractors.B: Assigning to one of many classes is multiclass classification, not binary.
distractors.C: Predicting continuous values is regression, not classification.
distractors.D: Grouping unlabeled data is clustering (unsupervised learning), not classification.

questions.stem: What distinguishes a regression problem from a classification problem?

options.text: Regression predicts mathematically continuous target values; classification predicts discrete categorical values
Regression uses unlabeled data; classification uses labeled data
Regression is unsupervised; classification is supervised
Regression only works with text data; classification only with images

explanation.correct: Regression predicts continuous values (house prices, temperatures). Classification predicts discrete categories (spam/not spam, disease type). Both are supervised learning with labeled data.

distractors.B: Both regression and classification use labeled data. They are both supervised learning problems.
distractors.C: Both are supervised learning. Unsupervised learning is clustering/anomaly detection without labels.
distractors.D: Both work with various data types. The distinction is continuous vs discrete output, not data modality.

questions.stem: Which are types of classification problems? (Select TWO)

options.text: Binary classification — assigning input to one of two mutually exclusive classes (e.g., disease or no disease)
Multiclass classification — assigning input to one of several classes (e.g., topic of a document: religion, politics, finance)
Continuous classification — predicting a number between 0 and infinity
Temporal classification — classifying data by timestamp
Spatial classification — classifying data by geographic location

explanation.correct: Classification has two main types: binary (two mutually exclusive classes) and multiclass (several possible classes). Binary example: disease/no disease. Multiclass example: document topic classification.

distractors.C: Predicting continuous numbers is regression, not classification.
distractors.D: Temporal classification is not a standard ML problem type. Time series is separate.
distractors.E: Spatial classification is not a standard type. Geographic grouping would be clustering.

questions.stem: Which are types of regression problems? (Select THREE)

options.text: Simple linear regression — single independent variable predicting a dependent variable
Multiple linear regression — multiple independent variables predicting a dependent variable
Logistic regression — predicting the probability of an event occurring (value between 0 and 1)
Circular regression — predicting values that repeat in cycles
Quantum regression — predicting quantum states
Emotional regression — predicting human emotions numerically

explanation.correct: Regression types include simple linear (one variable), multiple linear (several variables), and logistic (probability between 0 and 1 using logarithmic functions). All require significant labeled data.

distractors.D: Circular regression is not a standard ML problem type.
distractors.E: Quantum regression is not a recognized ML problem type.
distractors.F: Emotion detection is a classification or NLP task, not 'emotional regression.'

questions.stem: Which are unsupervised learning problem types? (Select TWO)

options.text: Clustering — discovering discrete groupings within data where members of groups are similar to each other
Anomaly detection — identifying rare items or observations that differ significantly from the rest of the data
Binary classification — predicting one of two labeled classes
Linear regression — predicting continuous values
Logistic regression — predicting probabilities from labeled data

explanation.correct: Clustering (grouping similar data points) and anomaly detection (finding rare observations that differ significantly from the rest) are unsupervised problems — they work with unlabeled data.

distractors.C: Binary classification requires labels (supervised). Unsupervised problems have no target labels.
distractors.D: Linear regression uses labeled data (supervised). It predicts values from known input-output pairs.
distractors.E: Logistic regression is supervised — it needs labeled examples of events and non-events.

questions.stem: What defines a clustering problem? (Select TWO)

options.text: Data objects are classified into groups where members are as similar as possible to each other
Members of different groups are as different as possible from each other
Each data point must have a pre-assigned label before clustering
Clustering requires continuous target values
Clustering can only work with image data

explanation.correct: Clustering groups data so members within a group are as similar as possible to each other and as different as possible from members of other groups. You define similarity features and specify the number of clusters.

distractors.C: Clustering is unsupervised — it doesn't need pre-assigned labels. It discovers groups from unlabeled data.
distractors.D: Continuous targets define regression. Clustering finds groupings without predicting numerical values.
distractors.E: Clustering works with any data type — customer purchase history, network traffic, text, images, etc.

questions.stem: What characterizes logistic regression? (Select TWO)

options.text: It predicts the probability of an event occurring with a value between 0 (unlikely) and 1 (maximum likelihood)
It uses logarithmic functions to compute the regression line
It predicts continuous values like house prices
It doesn't require any labeled training data
It can only use one independent variable

explanation.correct: Logistic regression predicts probability (0 to 1) of an event using logarithmic functions. Examples: predicting heart disease based on BMI/smoking/genetics, or predicting fraud probability.

distractors.C: Continuous values (prices) are linear regression. Logistic predicts probability between 0 and 1.
distractors.D: Logistic regression requires significant labeled data (fraud/not fraud, disease/no disease).
distractors.E: Logistic regression can use one or multiple independent variables.

questions.scenario: A bank needs to automatically approve or reject loan applications. Each application should receive exactly one of these two outcomes based on the applicant's financial attributes.
questions.stem: What type of ML problem is this?

options.text: Binary classification — assigning each application to one of two mutually exclusive classes (approved or rejected)
Multiclass classification — the application could be categorized into many outcome types
Regression — predicting the exact dollar amount to approve
Clustering — grouping similar applications together

explanation.correct: Approve/reject is a binary classification problem — two predefined mutually exclusive classes. The model assigns each application to one class based on financial attributes.

distractors.B: With only two outcomes (approve/reject), this is binary, not multiclass.
distractors.C: Predicting the approval amount would be regression. But approve/reject is a categorical decision.
distractors.D: Clustering groups without labels. Here we have a specific target (approve/reject) with training examples.

questions.scenario: A real estate company wants to predict the selling price of houses based on features like number of bedrooms, square footage, lot size, and location.
questions.stem: What type of ML problem is this?

options.text: Multiple linear regression — multiple independent variables (features) predicting a continuous dependent variable (price)
Binary classification — predicting if the house will sell or not
Clustering — grouping houses by similarity
Anomaly detection — finding unusual house listings

explanation.correct: Predicting a continuous value (price) from multiple features (bedrooms, sq ft, lot, location) is multiple linear regression. Multiple independent variables predict a mathematically continuous target.

distractors.B: Sell/not-sell would be binary classification. But the target here is the price (continuous), not a yes/no outcome.
distractors.C: Clustering groups data without a target variable. Here there's a specific target (price) to predict.
distractors.D: Anomaly detection finds outliers. The goal here is predicting specific price values.

questions.scenario: A manufacturing plant has sensors monitoring equipment temperature, vibration, and pressure. They want the system to automatically flag readings that are significantly different from normal patterns to prevent equipment failure.
questions.stem: What type of ML problem is this?

options.text: Anomaly detection — identifying observations that differ significantly from the rest of the data to detect potential failures
Classification — categorizing each reading as normal or abnormal with labeled data
Regression — predicting the exact time of equipment failure
Clustering — grouping sensors by type

explanation.correct: Identifying rare observations that differ significantly from normal patterns is anomaly detection. It's used to detect failed sensors, equipment issues, or security incidents by spotting deviations.

distractors.B: Classification requires pre-labeled normal/abnormal data. Anomaly detection discovers deviations from patterns without labels for every possible failure mode.
distractors.C: Predicting exact failure time would be regression. Anomaly detection flags unusual readings for investigation.
distractors.D: Grouping sensors by type is organizational. The goal is detecting unusual readings, not grouping sensors.

questions.scenario: An e-commerce company wants to segment their millions of customers into distinct groups based on purchase history and browsing behavior, without predefined categories.
questions.stem: What type of ML problem is this?

options.text: Clustering — discovering discrete groupings within customer data where members of each group share similar behaviors
Binary classification — customers are either active or inactive
Regression — predicting customer lifetime value
Multiclass classification — assigning customers to predefined segments

explanation.correct: Segmenting customers into groups by purchase history or behavior without predefined categories is clustering — an unsupervised technique that discovers natural groupings based on similarity.

distractors.B: Active/inactive would be binary classification with labels. Here there are no predefined categories.
distractors.C: CLTV prediction would be regression. The goal here is discovering groups, not predicting values.
distractors.D: Multiclass requires predefined labeled categories. Clustering discovers groups from unlabeled data.

questions.scenario: A healthcare AI team builds a model to predict whether a patient will develop heart disease. They use BMI, smoking status, and genetic predisposition as inputs and want a probability score between 0 and 1.
questions.stem: What type of regression model is appropriate?

options.text: Logistic regression — predicts probability of an event (heart disease) occurring, outputting a value between 0 and 1
Simple linear regression — predicts a continuous numerical value
Multiple linear regression — predicts house prices from features
Polynomial regression — fits a curved line to data points

explanation.correct: Logistic regression predicts the probability of an event (disease) using multiple independent variables (BMI, smoking, genetics). It outputs 0-1, where 0 = unlikely and 1 = maximum likelihood.

distractors.B: Simple linear regression uses one variable and predicts continuous values, not probabilities between 0-1.
distractors.C: Multiple linear regression predicts continuous values. Logistic regression predicts probability.
distractors.D: Polynomial regression fits curves for continuous prediction. Logistic uses logarithmic functions for probability.

questions.scenario: A business executive is evaluating which problems in their organization would benefit from AI.
questions.stem: Which scenarios make AI a good choice? (Select THREE)

options.text: Detecting fraud by recognizing patterns and deviations in millions of transactions
Reducing waste by forecasting demand for products or resources
Performing repetitive tedious tasks that employees struggle with or find boring
Making a one-time decision that can be solved with a simple spreadsheet formula
Calculating the sum of a column of numbers
Following a fixed set of business rules that never change

explanation.correct: AI excels at: fraud detection (pattern recognition in vast data), demand forecasting (reducing waste), and repetitive tasks (freeing employees). These leverage AI's strengths in scale, speed, and consistency.

distractors.D: A simple spreadsheet formula is deterministic and doesn't need AI. Use the simplest tool for the job.
distractors.E: Addition is a trivial calculation. AI is for complex pattern recognition, not basic arithmetic.
distractors.F: Fixed rules that never change should use rule-based systems. AI is for adapting and learning from data.

questions.scenario: A CTO is deciding which projects should NOT use ML and would be better served by traditional approaches.
questions.stem: In which situations is ML NOT the best approach? (Select TWO)

options.text: When deterministic output is required — the system must always produce the same result for the same input
When complete model transparency is required but only complex neural networks can solve the problem
When analyzing millions of records for fraud patterns
When predicting demand from historical sales data
When personalizing recommendations based on user behavior

explanation.correct: Avoid ML when determinism is needed (use rules instead) or when transparency requirements conflict with model complexity (complex models are black boxes). Rule-based systems are better in these cases.

distractors.C: Fraud detection from millions of records is an ideal AI use case — pattern recognition at scale.
distractors.D: Demand prediction from historical data is perfect for ML regression models.
distractors.E: Personalized recommendations are a core AI strength — learning patterns from user behavior.

questions.scenario: A data scientist is helping business teams identify what type of ML problem they have based on their data and goals.
questions.stem: How do you determine if a problem is supervised or unsupervised? (Select TWO)

options.text: If the dataset has labeled target values (known outputs), it is a supervised learning problem
If the dataset has features but no labels, and you need to discover patterns, it is an unsupervised problem
Supervised problems never use any input data
Unsupervised problems always require labeled data
The distinction depends on which AWS service you use

explanation.correct: Supervised = features with labeled targets (classification or regression). Unsupervised = features without labels, where you discover patterns (clustering, anomaly detection).

distractors.C: Supervised problems use both inputs (features) and outputs (labels). They require data.
distractors.D: Unsupervised is specifically characterized by NOT having labels. Labels make it supervised.
distractors.E: Problem type depends on data characteristics (labeled vs unlabeled), not which service is used.

questions.scenario: A credit union has a simple policy: automatically approve loans of $10,000 or less for any applicant with a credit score above 750. They want to automate this.
questions.stem: Should they use ML or a rule-based system for this automation?

options.text: A rule-based system — the logic is deterministic, simple, and doesn't require learning from data patterns
A deep learning neural network for maximum accuracy
An unsupervised clustering model to group applicants
A generative AI model to write approval letters

explanation.correct: This is a simple deterministic rule (score > 750 AND amount ≤ $10,000 → approve). Rule-based systems are appropriate when logic is simple, fixed, and deterministic. No ML pattern recognition is needed.

distractors.B: A neural network is overkill for a simple threshold rule. It adds complexity without benefit.
distractors.C: Clustering discovers groups in unlabeled data. The policy is a clear deterministic rule, not a pattern discovery problem.
distractors.D: The task is approval logic, not content generation. A rule engine handles the decision efficiently.

questions.scenario: A news organization wants to automatically categorize incoming articles into topics: politics, sports, technology, entertainment, and science.
questions.stem: What type of ML problem is this?

options.text: Multiclass classification — assigning each article to one of several predefined topic classes
Binary classification — articles are either news or not news
Regression — predicting how many people will read the article
Anomaly detection — finding unusual articles

explanation.correct: Assigning articles to one of several predefined classes (politics, sports, tech, entertainment, science) is multiclass classification — multiple possible discrete categories.

distractors.B: With five categories, this is multiclass, not binary. Binary would only have two options.
distractors.C: Predicting readership numbers would be regression. Topic assignment is classification.
distractors.D: Anomaly detection finds outliers. The goal is categorizing all articles, not finding unusual ones.
